title: Englisch C1 — BEC Higher Sample Test 2 (Reading & Listening)
subtitle: Cambridge English Business Higher (BEC Higher / B2 Business Vantage), Sample Paper 2

examInfo.date: Cambridge English Business Higher — Sample Paper 2 (Practice)
examInfo.format: Reading: 6 parts (multiple matching, gapped text, multiple choice, multiple-choice cloze, open cloze, error correction) · Listening: 3 parts (note completion, multiple matching, multiple choice)
examInfo.grading: Original Cambridge mark scheme: each question = 1 mark. This practice version scores Reading + Listening only (Writing & Speaking papers not included).

sections.title: Reading Part One: Multiple Matching
sections.description: Look at the statements below and at the five paragraphs (A–E) from an article about human capital management. Which paragraph does each statement refer to? You will need to use some letters more than once.
sections.context: A In an increasingly service-orientated global economy, more and more organisations have to have large numbers of employees covering frontline roles 24 hours a day. For those organisations, the workforce accounts for a large proportion of costs. The way it is managed and utilised is a critical success factor and fundamental to profitability and growth. Managers can ensure that the large workforces on which they depend remain motivated and productive, but this can only be achieved by facilitating closer collaboration between operations, HR and IT professionals within the organisation. B Workforce management software exists which integrates with finance and payroll systems, providing a comprehensive picture of human resources. Using it properly enables organisations to take a more holistic approach to the way they contract with and optimise the time of their employees. This approach is often described as 'human capital management'. Surprisingly, many organisations already have much of this functionality or, at least, the potential to develop it within their existing software systems. However, this doesn't always happen, largely due to poor internal collaboration, especially between the operations, HR and IT functions of the organisation. C When trying to achieve the most efficient and economic deployment of people, it's vital to remember that efficiency and economy are not necessarily the same thing. Having the right number of people working is one aspect, but rates of pay must also be taken into account. The key is to get a cross-functional team working on the way labour costs are measured and controlled. Budget variance reports must be used with caution – just because a department is below budget, it does not mean that the workforce is being managed as effectively as possible. D A more sophisticated approach requires information in the payroll system, such as time and attendance records and wage payments, to be combined with information in operational systems, such as service requirements. This allows for the analysis of planned and actual expenditure and enables managers to drill down and discover reasons for overspending. Even well-managed organisations have shaved 1–2% off labour costs with this approach. That may not seem much, but in the current climate it can mean the difference between profit and loss for labour-intensive companies. E Many organisations think they know what their optimum workforce is, but reviewing the data often exposes false assumptions based on outdated methods of assessing resourcing levels. Only when the organisation understands the dynamics of its labour expenditure will it be in a position to move towards 'workforce optimisation'. This is a more strategic challenge and demands a greater input from the HR function. Changes of many kinds can be made that lead to a more flexible, satisfied workforce with resulting improvements in service performance.

questions.gappedText:
1. Making a saving could prove crucially important in some instances. {{gap_1}}

2. A distinction must be made between two requirements. {{gap_2}}

3. Data drawn from different departments can give a useful overview. {{gap_3}}

4. Companies will only thrive if they deploy their workforce effectively. {{gap_4}}

5. Close examination shows that a belief is unfounded. {{gap_5}}

6. Reliance on a particular tool may lead to a false conclusion. {{gap_6}}

7. A particular method should be used to reveal underlying inefficiencies. {{gap_7}}

8. Organisations sometimes fail to take advantage of available resources. {{gap_8}}

gaps.explanation: Statement 1 corresponds to paragraph D.
Statement 2 corresponds to paragraph C.
Statement 3 corresponds to paragraph B.
Statement 4 corresponds to paragraph A.
Statement 5 corresponds to paragraph E.
Statement 6 corresponds to paragraph C.
Statement 7 corresponds to paragraph D.
Statement 8 corresponds to paragraph B.

sections.title: Reading Part Two: Gapped Text
sections.description: Read the article about finding business premises. Choose the best sentence (A–H) from the list to fill each gap. Do not use any letter more than once.
sections.context: A The trick is to allow for how it's all going to look in two or three years' time. B However, there is a premium to pay, which is the landlord's profit. C And your workplace can also play an important part in your company image. D This is because moving plant and machinery can be extremely costly. E If residential property is all about location, with commercial property a more subtle mix of factors comes into play: location, specification, negotiation. F Do not be persuaded into taking a long lease without breaks. G Unfortunately, businesses almost invariably neglect to market themselves. H They do not want to commit to a long-term mortgage at a time of great uncertainty.

questions.gappedText:
Finding a workplace

Now that you are in business, you will need a decent workplace. For most companies, property is the second-highest overhead, after staff, and in a smaller company it can be the highest. But frequently people do not treat the issue of finding the right workplace with the respect it deserves. In doing so, they expose themselves to all sorts of liabilities. Perhaps the first step is to decide whether you are going to rent or buy. Not many start-ups buy a property. (0) … H … Buying commercial property can be a great investment, but now is probably not the time to do it. That leaves a choice between a long-term lease and serviced accommodation. This is a question of balancing cost against flexibility. With serviced offices, all your outgoings – rent, business rates, bills, repairs and furnishings – are generally wrapped up into a monthly charge. {{gap_9}} With a conventional leasehold, on the other hand, the main benefits are lower outgoings and the opportunity to fit out the property in line with your needs. Finding the right home for your business is not the same as choosing a place to live. {{gap_10}} If you are a retailer, clearly, you will want to find a spot on the high street, but if yours is an office-based business, location is far less critical. You need to have a clear idea of what your requirements are, in terms of size, facilities, infrastructure and planning consents. Size is a difficult issue for any growing business. As a rough guide, you need about 6 square metres per person in a call centre, and 12–13 square metres per person in an office environment. {{gap_11}} Business parks and industrial estates often have the advantage that they can provide room to expand when you need it. Now to the thorny business of the lease itself and its negotiation. {{gap_12}} So don't make that mistake. Show that you are a sound bet, with references, accounts, bankers and so on. The landlord may only be concerned about the rent, but if you make your company attractive, the landlord is far more likely to negotiate a lease on equitable terms. You can sometimes get money off by negotiation, but it is often better to get the other terms right. {{gap_13}} Since the late 1980s, typical periods have come down from 20 years or more to an average of 6.4 years today. However, longer leases can be more appealing to industrial businesses. {{gap_14}} Also, the capital outlay for fitting out can be written down over a longer period.

gaps.explanation: Sentence B follows on from the description of the all-inclusive monthly charge — 'there is a premium to pay'.
Sentence E introduces the contrast between residential property (location) and commercial property (location, specification, negotiation).
Sentence A follows the discussion of size requirements — allow for how it will look in 2-3 years.
Sentence G explains why landlords need convincing — businesses neglect to market themselves.
Sentence F warns against long leases without breaks, leading into the point about lease lengths shortening.
Sentence D explains why longer leases suit industrial businesses (moving plant/machinery is costly).

sections.title: Reading Part Three: Multiple Choice Reading Comprehension
sections.description: Read the article about good leadership in business and choose the best answer (A, B, C or D) for each question.
sections.context:
There has been a trend in recent years for companies to take 'expert' advice on a variety of core business matters such as mission statements, values, and corporate and social responsibility policies and strategy. In many cases, however, companies go well beyond legitimate and desirable benchmarking or acquiring critical knowledge. What they do may even amount to the outsourcing of the management thought process and of management's engagement with staff and others. In my opinion, this is unhealthy, because it tends to remove managers from the effects of their actions. The role of management – despite, or maybe because of, the increasing complexity of business – is to provide the flexible glue that holds together all the pieces that make up an organisation and its environment, from shareholders to staff, and from competitive forces to markets.

To be the glue in this context, leaders need significant abilities. They include the normally touted skills, such as clear communication – although many firms outsource control of their communications to a public relations agency, which ends up writing the script. But perhaps more important is the often neglected skill of understanding the complex interplay between interested groups of people. This encompasses an ability to understand their strategic interests as well as reading the individuals that you're dealing with – and having the personal and interpersonal skills to get results.

Such astuteness is not about doing enough staff surveys or producing lots of newsletters – although these have their place. It's about management teams having empathy for the groups they work with. Such understanding of the character of an organisation is vital and provides a solid base for progress. If managers have an in-depth understanding of the issues that interest, bother or motivate their staff, they will be far more successful at engaging people to respond to the future challenges of the organisation. As a result, progress is more likely to include the majority than the minority.

Every company has a character that is a product of its history and its current staff, a set of underlying values and behaviours which may not coincide with those claimed in the annual report. Often, these emerge in times of stress – they provide the default position when people are being made redundant or pension plans are being changed. Understanding this is vital. Woe betide any manager who can't sense how an organisation is feeling.

Such deep values can change, but this rarely happens by revolution. For most organisations, it is evolution – often gentle but persistent – that is likely to carry change further. Too many managers write a decisive 'big change' board paper rather than a potentially far more effective recommendation for incremental progress. Regardless of whether it's incremental or sudden, though, change – and hopefully progress – is more likely to be effective and take hold if the leaders of an organisation understand and are sensitive to the underlying values. For me, this is a way in which leaders can be most valuable: by appraising the shifting behaviour of interacting groups of people when they are made uncomfortable.

The mistake is for company leaders to base every pronouncement on the indexing of staff attitudes, corporate social responsibility benchmarking and a multitude of CEO blogs and corporate communications. In my view, leadership is about judgement and about being respectful to existing values. Leadership decisions may be manifested in a speech or even a blog, but the communication must be the consequence of a management team's thoughts and deliberations. Formulaic and clumsy responses are just no use.

questions.text: What does the writer criticise in the first paragraph?

choices.text: a lack of consistency in styles of outsourcing
the attitude of some managers towards outsourcing
over-reliance by management on consultants
the increasing emphasis on such issues as mission statements

questions.explanation: The writer criticises companies that go 'well beyond legitimate and desirable benchmarking' and effectively outsource 'the management thought process' to outside experts/consultants.

questions.text: What opinion does the writer express in the second paragraph?

choices.text: The skills that are most useful to managers are often undervalued.
The strategic interests of different groups often conflict with each other.
The best advice on communications comes from public relations agencies.
The ability to communicate effectively in writing is essential for managers.

questions.explanation: The writer says that 'perhaps more important is the often neglected skill of understanding the complex interplay between interested groups of people' — an undervalued but crucial skill.

questions.text: According to the third paragraph, understanding the character of the organisation helps managers to

choices.text: produce good quality internal communications.
identify the best way to consult the staff.
ensure they employ the most suitable staff.
gain staff co-operation in a changing environment.

questions.explanation: Managers who understand staff concerns 'will be far more successful at engaging people to respond to the future challenges of the organisation' — i.e. gain co-operation in a changing environment.

questions.text: What point is made about company culture in the fourth paragraph?

choices.text: It should be presented accurately in the annual report.
It can be seen in the employees' responses to problems.
It is more strongly influenced by managers than other staff.
It may help to minimise the effects of stress.

questions.explanation: 'these emerge in times of stress – they provide the default position when people are being made redundant or pension plans are being changed' — culture is revealed through employees' reactions to problems.

questions.text: What does the writer suggest in the fifth paragraph about introducing change?

choices.text: It is important to make the reasons for the change clear.
Companies often choose inappropriate ways of doing it.
Everyone who will be affected should be consulted first.
Values are most often changed by external influences.

questions.explanation: 'Too many managers write a decisive ‘big change’ board paper rather than a potentially far more effective recommendation for incremental progress' — companies often choose the wrong (overly dramatic) approach.

questions.text: According to the last paragraph, leaders should

choices.text: consider not only the message but also the style of delivery.
absorb information from many sources before making decisions.
think carefully about whether changes are necessary.
select the best medium for announcing changes.

questions.explanation: 'Leadership decisions may be manifested in a speech or even a blog, but the communication must be the consequence of a management team's thoughts and deliberations. Formulaic and clumsy responses are just no use' — both message and delivery style matter.

sections.title: Reading Part Four: Multiple-Choice Cloze
sections.description: Read the extract from the annual report of an oil exploration company. Choose the correct word to fill each gap from the four options.

questions.gappedText:
Tenex Oil — Annual Report

At Tenex our strategy of delivering growth both from the production base and from high-potential exploration is backed by a solid financial position. Existing cash {{gap_21}}, bank borrowing facilities and strong cash flows will allow us to fund existing and new development projects. Our year-end net cash position of US$79.0 million {{gap_22}} strong cash generation and a disciplined {{gap_23}} to expenditure. Our professionals use the latest technological tools to keep us at the forefront of the industry and we employ the best available specialists in all of the projects that we {{gap_24}}. We manage our portfolio to ensure that our focus is on the best prospects. For our development projects, we consider all the possible options, including appropriate new technology and {{gap_25}} the range of possible outcomes before selecting the optimal design. We {{gap_26}} considerable value on the creativity of our geologists, geophysicists and engineers. It is this that enables us to work in previously overlooked regions, so that, {{gap_27}} with new scientific concepts, innovative technologies and state-of-the-art equipment, we can operate in such regions more successfully than in the past. Tenex aspires to be an industry leader in social performance, which covers the areas of social responsibility, health and safety, and environmental impact. Our {{gap_28}} policy is to ensure that the risks and impacts of our activities are reduced to 'as low as reasonably practicable' at all times. We fix targets with {{gap_29}} to our historical performance, the performance of our peer group and standards {{gap_30}} by external agencies.

gaps.explanation: 'resources' is the correct word/collocation in this context.
'reflects' is the correct word/collocation in this context.
'approach' is the correct word/collocation in this context.
'undertake' is the correct word/collocation in this context.
'assess' is the correct word/collocation in this context.
'place' is the correct word/collocation in this context.
'armed' is the correct word/collocation in this context.
'stated' is the correct word/collocation in this context.
'reference' is the correct word/collocation in this context.
'set' is the correct word/collocation in this context.

sections.title: Reading Part Five: Open Cloze
sections.description: Read the article about team-building training courses. Type ONE word for each gap. (Capitalisation does not matter.)

questions.gappedText:
Team-building keeps staff on side

If you're asked to organise a team-building course, the first thing to do is to decide what you hope to achieve. Secondly, plan for the follow-up. Courses to improve communication and boost morale are {{gap_31}} the increase. A company called GRY, for example, has recently started offering open-air courses which seek to bring {{gap_32}} barriers and build trust. Company founder Henry Blackwell says: 'It goes without saying that team-building events are popular with participants, but, in my experience, nearly {{gap_33}} motivational weekends treat the symptoms of a problem, {{gap_34}} the root cause. Research has highlighted that 94 percent of employees aren't doing what they want to be doing in their work. Unless organisations tackle this issue, investment in team-building will be ineffective.' Piers Lawson, director of A-Event, agrees: 'It can be difficult to treat a problem that is deep-rooted, but if a company is serious about rebuilding a team, particularly {{gap_35}} that involves a group of managers or people who work in the same department, we have contacts who can assist.' However, he admits that the majority of clients simply require a fun day or weekend during {{gap_36}} staff can get to know their colleagues outside the office. Piers points out that in the past, by {{gap_37}} the most significant indicator of a company's success {{gap_38}} its financial resources, but this has now shifted to its employees: 'It's about attracting {{gap_39}} right talent and ensuring these people learn to work as a team. You also have to decide what the team should be doing after the event that they are not doing now, and set your budget some time {{gap_40}} you think about seeking quotes from course providers.'

gaps.explanation: 'are on the increase'.
'bring down barriers'.
'nearly all motivational weekends'.
'treat the symptoms... not the root cause'.
'particularly one that involves...'.
'a weekend during which staff can get to know...'.
'by far the most significant indicator'.
'the most significant indicator... was its financial resources' (past tense).
'attracting the right talent'.
'set your budget some time before you think about seeking quotes'.

sections.title: Reading Part Six: Error Correction
sections.description: Most lines of the text below contain one extra word which is grammatically incorrect or does not fit the sense of the text. For each numbered line, type that extra word. Some lines are correct — for these, type CORRECT.

questions.gappedText:
Winning new customers

In most of the lines there is one extra word which is grammatically incorrect or does not fit the sense of the text. Some lines are correct. If a line is correct, type CORRECT. If there is an extra word, type that word.

0 New customers are the oxygen every business needs to grow. But unless customer — (example: UNLESS)
00 acquisition remains an enigma to many business owners: they just don't — (example: CORRECT)

41 know where to start when it comes to try finding new customers. The problem many {{gap_41}}
42 have is that after they exhaust their contact base and then do not know where {{gap_42}}
43 to go next. Word of mouth will only get you so far. You need take a strategic {{gap_43}}
44 approach. Getting new clients often depends on understanding your existing ones first. {{gap_44}}
45 Only by then, once you've analysed the real motivation of each of your customers, {{gap_45}}
46 can you acquire new ones. And that is where many businesses go out wrong. They {{gap_46}}
47 are so busy scanning the horizon for new prospects, they equally fail to look at existing {{gap_47}}
48 relationships. So, do research into customers' buying patterns and learn from it {{gap_48}}
49 marketing campaigns that have been successful. Realisation of what makes customers to {{gap_49}}
50 tick will give you insights into the new market you are aiming at. Put yourself {{gap_50}}
51 in the place of being the customer – what sales message would you want to hear? Of {{gap_51}}
52 course it is rarely easy, but if by looking at your business with the eyes of a customer, {{gap_52}}
...you are better prepared to expand your market.

gaps.explanation: The extra word is 'try'.
The extra word is 'after'.
The extra word is 'take'.
This line is correct as written.
The extra word is 'by'.
The extra word is 'out'.
The extra word is 'equally'.
The extra word is 'it'.
The extra word is 'to'.
This line is correct as written.
The extra word is 'being'.
The extra word is 'if'.

sections.title: Listening Part One: Note Completion
sections.description: You will hear a business lecturer talking to a group of students about a marketing technique called 'closed-loop marketing'. As you listen, complete the notes using up to three words or a number. You can play the recording as many times as you like.

questions.gappedText:
CLOSED-LOOP MARKETING

Current problems faced by companies:
Recent developments have led to the {{gap_1}} of products.
The money allocated to {{gap_2}} may be the main difference between competitors.
Consumers receive a wide range of marketing {{gap_3}} which they are paying less and less attention to.
Consumers' {{gap_4}} appears to be falling.
Consumers are becoming more {{gap_5}} in their buying behaviour.
Companies are unable to make good use of all the {{gap_6}} they have.

Closed-loop marketing:
• constantly monitors consumers' {{gap_7}} to a marketing campaign
• provides communication with consumers through various kinds of {{gap_8}}
• enabled Global Beach to identify which {{gap_9}} had the greatest effect on consumers
• gave Jaguar a much higher {{gap_10}} when contacting potential customers
• reduces the amount of {{gap_11}}, which used to be included in marketing budgets
• helps companies to make strategic decisions
• will reduce the quantity of {{gap_12}} that consumers receive.

gaps.explanation: 'there is now a massive problem of almost all products being in oversupply'.
'the only point of differentiation... lies in their advertising spend'.
'companies bombard consumers with a multiplicity of messages'.
'satisfaction is plunging among consumers'.
'they are increasingly unpredictable in their reactions to advertising'.
'investing heavily in databases... they're drowning in data'.
'the response of targeted consumers can be continuously monitored'.
'Closed-loop marketing employs electronic media, such as the internet, call centres...'.
'Global Beach tested and refined a range of incentives'.
'a 16 per cent conversion rate from registering an interest to taking a test drive'.
'closed-loop marketing gives the opportunity to minimise the waste that was previously built into marketing budgets'.
'consumers will have far fewer junk e-mails to contend with'.

sections.title: Listening Part Two: Multiple Matching
sections.description: You will hear five people talking about change and growth in the companies where they work. For Task One, choose the change under consideration each speaker mentions (A–H). For Task Two, choose the obstacle to future growth each speaker identifies (A–H).
sections.context: TASK ONE — CHANGE UNDER CONSIDERATION (for gaps marked 'Change') A introduction of new training provision B improvement of performance appraisal process C establishment of new section D implementation of automated costings E creation of an additional layer of management F extension of internet access G adoption of new system for handling data H revision of budget for business travel TASK TWO — OBSTACLES TO FUTURE GROWTH (for gaps marked 'Obstacle') A short-term planning of IT needs B lack of qualifications among staff C too much separation between departments D inefficiency of delivery contractors E ineffectiveness of recruitment strategy F low motivation among management G poor quality of some components H reluctance to reduce the number of senior staff

questions.gappedText:
Speaker 1: Change {{gap_13}}   |   Obstacle {{gap_18}}
Speaker 2: Change {{gap_14}}   |   Obstacle {{gap_19}}
Speaker 3: Change {{gap_15}}   |   Obstacle {{gap_20}}
Speaker 4: Change {{gap_16}}   |   Obstacle {{gap_21}}
Speaker 5: Change {{gap_17}}   |   Obstacle {{gap_22}}

gaps.explanation: Speaker 1's change under consideration corresponds to option G.
Speaker 2's change under consideration corresponds to option H.
Speaker 3's change under consideration corresponds to option F.
Speaker 4's change under consideration corresponds to option E.
Speaker 5's change under consideration corresponds to option B.
Speaker 1's obstacle to growth corresponds to option A.
Speaker 2's obstacle to growth corresponds to option H.
Speaker 3's obstacle to growth corresponds to option D.
Speaker 4's obstacle to growth corresponds to option C.
Speaker 5's obstacle to growth corresponds to option F.

sections.title: Listening Part Three: Multiple Choice
sections.description: You will hear part of a conversation between two colleagues, Kelly and Daniel, who work in the marketing department of a company. They are talking about product packaging. For each question, choose the best answer A, B or C.

questions.text: Kelly believes that their company's packaging

choices.text: should use materials that use fewer natural resources.
needs to help to make people's lives more convenient.
can contribute significantly to their products' success.

questions.explanation: 'If we get it right, it'll help us turn over huge sales of the new range.'

questions.text: According to Daniel, the main challenge of designing packaging for their new range is

choices.text: convincing consumers of the practical reason for its format.
finding a solution that avoids an increase in production costs.
producing an innovative idea without extensive market research.

questions.explanation: 'the test will be to persuade our target market that there's a credible justification for choosing that particular packaging type.'

questions.text: Kelly feels it would be advantageous to use their packaging in order to

choices.text: emphasise customer service guarantees.
underline the product benefits to customers.
encourage customers to contact the company.

questions.explanation: 'We should be using that to establish a real dialogue and collect useful feedback' — encouraging customers to get in touch.

questions.text: Daniel feels that packaging is the most powerful marketing tool because it can

choices.text: create sectors within which to develop brands.
add value to a range of different products.
impact on consumers' everyday lives.

questions.explanation: 'It can bring whole sectors into existence allowing products and brands to evolve.'

questions.text: Kelly thinks the Australian company Smile used foil pouches for its children's drinks as a way to

choices.text: transfer the brand associations of other product types.
update its brand that was being overtaken by competitors.
promote a new brand image without changing product ingredients.

questions.explanation: 'By adopting the meaning of other product categories, they were able to underline important qualities, like freshness.'

questions.text: Daniel suggests that Smile is doing well now because its new packaging

choices.text: uses humour in a particularly effective way.
is the most noticeable on supermarket shelves.
reflects a growing public interest in healthy eating.

questions.explanation: 'what's really sparked this success is that they've capitalised on consumers' concerns for healthier products.'

questions.text: Kelly believes the redesign of the Smile brand is particularly clever because

choices.text: the graphics do not resemble those of any other similar products.
the company's core values are being promoted via the packaging.
the product has widened its appeal without losing loyal customers.

questions.explanation: 'It's awe-inspiring how they offer a rare glimpse into the philosophy of the people who make the product. There's a real social dimension that comes across.'

questions.text: Daniel feels one possible weakness of Smile's new packaging is that

choices.text: the product is no longer visible through the packaging material.
the styles used are not consistent across its product range.
the previous design fault with the cap has remained unsolved.

questions.explanation: 'they've used too many different styles in the graphics that don't look compatible with each other.'

_validation: Listening audio: original BEC Higher Sample Paper 2 recordings (mp3) included under assets/exams/c1-bec-higher-2/.
Writing and Speaking papers are not part of this practice exam.
Reading Part 3 source text was extracted from a two-column PDF layout and re-assembled into continuous paragraphs; content is unchanged.
Open cloze (Part 5) and error correction (Part 6) answers are checked case-insensitively; common alternative spellings are accepted where the official answer key lists them.
